_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Georgetown County, SC. Records from 1800 (county formation). The courthouse has not suffered catastrophic loss, and Georgetown's probate records are particularly valuable because the concentration of rice plantation wealth meant frequent estate proceedings that listed enslaved people.
display_name: Georgetown County, South Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

current_courthouse.location: Georgetown, SC — 129 Screven Street (one of the oldest continuously operating courthouses in SC, renovated multiple times)

previous_courthouses.location: Georgetown — the Georgetown District courthouse. Earlier than the county, the Georgetown Judicial District served the area from 1769.
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by the 1824 building. Records largely survived the transition.

courthouse_history.notes: Georgetown County was formed from Georgetown District in 1800 (though the district continued for some judicial functions until 1868). The courthouse dates from 1824 and has survived without catastrophic loss. Georgetown's records are particularly valuable because the extraordinary concentration of wealth in rice plantations generated extensive probate proceedings — estate inventories, guardianship records, and partition suits that name enslaved people and describe plantation operations in detail.

record_losses.cause: None — no catastrophic fire, war damage, or natural disaster
record_losses.description: Georgetown County's courthouse has not suffered a major loss. Records from the county's formation (1800) and from the earlier Georgetown District survive. The 1893 and later hurricanes caused no known damage to the courthouse. This makes Georgetown one of the better-preserved Lowcountry counties for genealogical research.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Georgetown's records survive — if {ancestor_name} owned land, appeared in court, or died with an estate in Georgetown County after 1800, the records are almost certainly there. The estate inventories, in particular, are among the most detailed in the state.
record_losses.source: SC Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records.

deeds.completeness: Excellent from county formation. Georgetown District land records (1769-1800) are at SCDAH.
deeds.gaps: No significant gaps. Colonial-era land grants are at SCDAH.
deeds.repository: Georgetown County Register of Deeds; SCDAH (district/colonial era)
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com SC land records

wills_estates.completeness: Excellent. Georgetown's probate records are rich because the rice plantation wealth generated frequent estate proceedings. Estate inventories routinely list enslaved people by name, with ages and valuations.
wills_estates.gaps: No significant gaps from county formation.

marriages.completeness: Statewide marriage licenses from 1911. Before that, church records — particularly All Saints Waccamaw (Episcopal), Prince George Winyah (Episcopal) — are the primary source.
marriages.repository: Georgetown County Probate Court; parish registers

court_minutes.repository: Georgetown County Clerk of Court; SCDAH

substitute_sources.description: Georgetown County's rice plantations generated extensive private records — account books, slave lists, overseer reports, correspondence, and plantation journals. The Allston, Weston, Sparkman, and other planter families' records survive in various archives.
substitute_sources.repository: SC Historical Society; USC South Caroliniana Library; Duke University Special Collections; Georgetown County Library
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Plantation records frequently name enslaved individuals, describe family units, record births and deaths, and document skills and task assignments. These records are among the most important sources for African American genealogy in Georgetown County.
substitute_sources.source: Joyner, Down by the Riverside (1984). Doar, Rice and Rice Planting (1936).

substitute_sources.source_type: Episcopal parish registers (All Saints Waccamaw, Prince George Winyah)
substitute_sources.description: The Episcopal parishes in Georgetown County — All Saints Waccamaw and Prince George Winyah — maintained baptism, marriage, and burial registers from the colonial era. These registers record both white parishioners and enslaved people who were baptized.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual parishes; SCDAH; SC Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Parish registers are the primary substitute for vital records before 1915. The All Saints Waccamaw register is particularly important for the Waccamaw Neck plantation district.

narrative_hooks: Georgetown's courthouse has stood since 1824 and the records inside go back to 1800 — {ancestor_name}'s deed, will, or estate inventory is likely there, preserved in one of the oldest courthouses in continuous use in South Carolina.

Georgetown's probate records are among the richest in the state because the rice plantation wealth generated constant estate proceedings — if {ancestor_name} was enslaved in Georgetown County, the estate inventory that valued {pronoun_object} as property may also preserve {pronoun_possessive} name.

The All Saints Waccamaw parish register — an Episcopal church in the heart of the rice district — may hold {ancestor_name}'s baptism or burial, recorded by a church that served both the planter families and the enslaved community.

If {ancestor_name} was a freedperson in Georgetown County after the war, the Freedmen's Bureau labor contracts may be the first place {pronoun_possessive} name appears as a free person in a government record.
source_references: SC Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records.
Joyner, Charles. Down by the Riverside. University of Illinois Press, 1984.
Doar, David. Rice and Rice Planting in the South Carolina Low Country. Charleston Museum, 1936.
SC Historical Society. Parish register collections and finding aids.
